speaker: interviewer
text: Schneid-Ofseyer. Can you please introduce yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly. My name is Corey Keeble, I'm one of the curatorial staff with the Royal Ontario Museum in Toronto, Ontario, Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you come to be associated with Miriam and Otto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I first met Miriam in the context of setting up exhibitions for the New European Judaica gallery at the Royal Ontario Museum. That was my first introduction to Miriam. And perhaps I should also mention that Miriam has been my mentor, as far as learning, um, Hebrew. She's been my Hebrew teacher. But it was through Miriam that we borrowed the Otto Schneid bronze bust of Schneid's mother, which we have used in the Judaica gallery at the Royal Ontario Museum as a commemoration for the Holocaust. I felt it was a singularly suitable sculpture, and it struck me that it was far more suitable as a document on the Holocaust than almost any other single artifact of which I could think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we will see the mother later on, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: --before we do, Miriam actually asked you to read this particular--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --short piece by Otto.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'The Man and the Squirrel.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us a bit about it before you start reading it? The significance of why we're reading it today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I suspect that the importance really becomes self-evident in the-- in the context of the text. 'The Man and the Squirrel' interview is a little more than just tete-a-tete. There is a certain element of tension. And it is quite clear that the potential danger, as far as the-- the script is concerned, comes from the man rather than from the squirrel. So there are questions of identity, questions of how we see one another, questions of understanding, of tolerance, or their inversions. [PAUSE FOR 3 SECONDS] Dialogue between man and squirrel. 'Come still nearer. I-- I won't do anything to you, don't be afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I-- I'm not, but, um, I-- I know many like you. You-- you-- you could change your mind. I-- I can't be quite without caution. You are so mighty and I can only jump away. This is the only thing I can do. Should I give up my only chance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aha, you're right. Many of us do things we should not, but I am a little different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Um, are you not like all of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Between humans, there are differences. One is human, the other is not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mmm, strange enough. All squirrels are squirrels. How can it be that not all humans are human?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it is difficult to be a human and easy to be a squirrel.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think so because maybe you were never a squirrel? I'm almost sure I never was. What difficulties have you? Don't you know? We're hungry, thirsty, and cold. We have terrible enemies. Cats? Owls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but, um, those are not the worst ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is the worst?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry, but, um, you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did I do to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, indeed, nothing. Um, maybe the so-called others do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you? Um, so far, nothing. Uh, but you slew my sister with a stone not long ago. She had always liked you and praised your kindness. Why did you do it? Were you hungry?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And I didn't do it. Very sorry, witnesses saw it. I'm not familiar with your differences, I only know that you are you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Listen, when you eat, does this, uh, satiate your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, I-- I indeed would like to taste something.

speaker: interviewer
text: I happen to have something for you. These nuts. What? Aren't they good? [SPITS] Sorry, no. Full of salt. Why did you spoil them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we like to change them a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand. You change everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe. Ourselves too. Once, we used to change very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you happier now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm. [SIGH] Not-- not so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why do you change so much?

speaker: interviewee
text: We can't help changing. You also seem to change.

speaker: interviewer
text: What? Change with me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, um, always thought you were brown. I've looked at you all this time and you're gray. Maybe there are more things not just as you thought.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you right with all that you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I hope not.

speaker: interviewer
text: How can you hope that you are not right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, I-- I think about something awful, thus I hope I'm not right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it that you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I would have never thought it if I had not been told it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, I-- I-- I don't know. A-- a-- a rumor came to me, um, l-like a visitor comes. Um, as you came.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it the rumor told?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, that you are going to kill all the squirrels.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really? I am going to? Why should I?

speaker: interviewee
text: Surely you have no reason. You had also no reason to slay my poor sister. But you are going to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How could I? You change so much. You must have something quite new for killing all of us. Afterwards, will you be happier?

speaker: interviewee
text: In no way. But believe me, I have no design like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm afraid you'll do it without design. I do hope I shall not. Are you such a human who is human, or one who is not human?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me think that over. Nice meeting you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nice living.' Can you tell us about the significance of it today, being what day it is today?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 8th, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Uh, well-- [PAUSE] actually, can we cut? Um, mm, mmm mmm mmm. [SIGH]

speaker: interviewer
text: The reason why I ask you that is today's the anniversary of the second dropping of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of Nagasaki.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the atomic bomb.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. Mm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that Otto was talking to the squirrel about the power of the atom bomb, or the Holocaust, or both?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think everything. It's, uh, it's man versus nature. That's part of it. Uh, you think? Is that fair game?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to thank you very much. Now we're going to go and look at the mother, and then afterwards, the monument to the 6 million.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. To go back to the question of Otto Schneid's dialogue, the discourse between the, uh, the man and the squirrel, the passage is one that is amenable to so many different interpretations on so many different levels that we would probably be here for hours, days, weeks. Because it, um, it really is a statement which, um, which deals with universal themes. Squirrel and man. Obviously, there is, um-- there is a sense of discord between the world of nature, the world of, uh, of human beings. The squirrel is small, defenseless. The man is, uh, physically larger, has-- has more power at his command. There is an obvious statement, I think, about, um, the human role in, um, destroying the environment. Destroying not merely animal species, but, uh, but the human destruction of, um, of other human beings. It's something that Otto Schneid was engaged with on many different levels, as far as his great concern about man's inhumanity to man. It was something that he experienced personally. It was something that, in a sense, he dedicated his life to-- to preventing, to stopping. And Otto Schneid, as much as so many other individuals in the 20th century, is witness to the appalling cat-- catastrophes that human beings have inflicted on one another, on the environment, on all other species. The fact that we have brought ourselves close to nuclear Armageddon-- this is the day, this taping day, of the 50th anniversary of the second atomic bomb, the bomb that was dropped on Nagasaki. And again, you can see that event as having a particular powerful implication, as far as the dialogue between man and squirrel. Here is the squirrel concerned with the threat to its own kind, and here is the man who, through science, through a misapplication of reason, has mastered the powers of the universe which threaten to destroy the entire human race and all other life forms on this planet. Now one of the difficulties with speaking of human catastrophe is that we tend to lose sight of what, I think, really is the most important reality of all, which is that we-- we do not deal with abstracts. I'll have to invoke the name, of all people, Joseph Stalin, because it was Stalin who said that the death of a single individual is a tragedy, whereas the death of a million people is a statistic. And it is this reality that, I think, makes one think with great seriousness about the image that Otto Schneid provided in the portrait of his own mother, who died in Auschwitz in 1943. The bust is an important statement-- [CLEARS THROAT] Excuse me. The bust is an important statement for several different reasons. One, of course, is that it is a portrait. It is a reminder of an individual. It tells us point blank, face to face, that the Holocaust is not a matter of statistics, that it is ultimately the story of millions of human individuals, each one with a life, with a history. Each one with-- with a soul. Each one with-- with feelings, with passions. This, I think, is the only way in which the Holocaust can really be approached. It is through the realization that we are confronting individuals. Individuals who died horribly for no reason other than human hatred, human bigotry, human callousness, human cruelty. There is another significance in the bust itself, because the bust of Otto Schneid's mother is a symbol of not the end of things, but really, of a beginning. It has a deliberate phoenix quality. Around the figure are stylized flickering flames, but these are not representative of an end. If you study the bust carefully, you'll see that there is an extraordinary serenity in the features of the face. And you will also see that just from the position of the bust, with the head tilted upwards towards the heavens, that this is a statement of triumph. This is a statement of victory. It is the triumph of the human soul. It is the victory of the human soul over the degradation of war, of intolerance, of cruelty, of mass murder. There is a phoenix quality, again, in the bust, which reminds us that the contributions of Jewish culture to European life and civilization are not something which can be exterminated. They are not something which can be brought to an end. But the bust itself says that-- says that life continues, that there is resurrection from the ashes, that the very spirit of life itself continues. And that the very spirit of Jewish life, of art, of civilization, of all of the things that the Jews of Europe have contributed not only to European life and culture, but to the life and culture of the world-- these things must continue. These things endure, these things will survive, and there will be no end, there will be no defeat. Instead, again, we're reminded of triumph, we're reminded of victory. It is, I think, one of the most extraordinarily eloquent works of its kind that I've seen. And it says something about the Holocaust, which is appropriate. It says something which needs to be said. And again, looking into those gentle features, one is reminded that here we have an image of a real person, of a real human being. A woman who was a mother, a woman who had children that she loved. The sense of compassion, the sense of soul, the sense of all the things that are truly human and which make life rich in its experience. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Otto Schneid's monument to the 6 millions is preserved in maquette form, in the form of a sculptural model. The significance of the monument, certainly in terms of its conception, is appropriately summarized by the words of the poet Gabriel Preil, who said the following, and I quote-- 'A summing up of these efforts is the model of a 'Monument of Death and Resurrection.' It is a pyramidal or rather conic structure, which rises up in two spirals opposing each other. Below are slopes crowded with images of agony and death. Above is shown the heroic revolt of partisans. Out of their bodies, the phoenix is being born, symbol of everlasting life and hope. In its mighty wings are embedded soaring figures. The multiplicity of figures, however, does not disrupt the almost monolithic unity of this all-embracing, nobly conceived, and dynamically visualized work. Its projected height, about 35 feet, brings up the optical problem of viewing such a formidable object from bottom to top. The artist solves it by planning a cylindrical pit in the ground out of which the monument would rise, reaching the level of the surroundings at half its height. In order to see the lower half, the viewer would descend on stairs leading him round and round to the base and back again to the outer level. The upper half would be seen from the outside of the encircling balustrade by walking around it. From no single point could the monument be viewed in its entirety. Thus, a work so dynamic in character must also be dynamically perceived and experienced. To the irrational concept corresponds a quite irrational process of seeing.' End of quotation. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Unfortunately, photographs do not do true justice to the memorial. Even in photographic form, it is essential to realize the completely plastic three dimensional nature of the composition. It is essential that the monument be seen from different angles, from different sides. This means that it has to be walked through. It has to be experienced in full 360 degrees of rotation. And it has to be seen either by walking around it or by walking through the interior. It's important to remember that in Schneid's actual design for the monument, the interior of the monument was to be a memorial sanctuary. The bronze was to be-- or at least the figure was to be made of bronze. The monument was intended to be of hollow cast bronze, and the center section of the hollow bronze casting was intended to serve as a memorial sanctuary, the space inside the sanctuary being the negative of the sculptural form. The plasticity of the monument, it seems, carries over concepts which can certainly be taken back to the period of the high and late Renaissance, and perhaps even to the concept of figura serpentinata, or multiple view sculpture, usually associated with Michelangelo, as far as the invention, but probably best realized-- appropriately, I think-- in bronze sculpture of the late 16th and early 17th century, associated with the workshop of Giovanni de Bologna, who was sculptor to the Medici and who was one of the primary exponents of multip-- multiple view sculpture at the time of the Renaissance. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What Otto Schneid has done with the monument to the 6 million has been to take this concept of completely three dimensional sculpture, and to carry it into a monumentality which was not achieved, not-- not reached by any of the sculptures of the-- of the 16th century. So the concept has aspects which are revolutionary, which are daring, which are highly experimental. But at the same time, it is anchored very firmly in traditions of European sculpture-- particularly European bronze sculpture-- which take us back in direct ascent to the sculptors of the Italian Renaissance.

speaker: interviewer
text: This painting by Otto Schneid is part of a tryptic. It's called 'Dance.' And it is not a turnable the painting, because you can turn it only upside down and downside up, but you cannot turn it to any other side. And you have here the man and the woman upside down, kind of shag-- in a [INAUDIBLE] way. And it's a dance. It's a dance between the two. Her dreams and his shyness. The two paintings that we are not going to see today have the sun, the moon, and the stars, and also musical instruments playing. [PAUSES for 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a 1965 painting by Otto Schneid. Oh, I'm sorry. 1961 painting by Otto Schneid. And it is 'Prayer at Moonlight.' You don't see the moonlight here. The moonlight is in the faces of the people. And you have different kind of simple people, simple Jews. Some of them are not interested at all in the prayer. They're concentrated in themselves. And if you see on the right, there is one that looks somewhere else. On the extreme left, there is somebody whose hand is covering his face. And the little child on the left is also looking somewhere else. But some of them are full of enthusiasm. That is a monumental painting and a complicated one, different from the other paintings that Otto did. It is called [INAUDIBLE], and it is based on the story in the Haggadah that in [INAUDIBLE] were sitting five scholars, among them Rabbi Akiva. And they were discussing that's what's written in the Haggadah, the exodus from Egypt, the whole night, until their pupils came and said to them, rabbis, it's now time for [INAUDIBLE]. In other words, it's morning, and you have to start praying. Some scholars thought that this is just because there was censorship. You couldn't tell the real thing that was happening. If you think about Rabbi Akiva, he was one of the heads of the rebellion against the Romans, and he was the one who elected Bar Kochba to fight against the Romans. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So this, in the-- in the eyes of Otto, is the preparation for their rebellion. On the Seder night, there-- there are sitting the five scholars. Four of them are in despair. The fifth, at the top of the table, is Rabbi Akiva. And he is sure that everything is going to be OK, because he thinks that Bar Kochba is the messiah, messiah.

speaker: interviewee
text: His friends told him, Akiva, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Akiva, you will die, and where you have cheeks, grass will start growing. A messiah will not come yet. But he still believed in them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here you have-- instead of saying army, ins-- instead of saying soldiers, you have here people who are the pupils of Rabbi Akiva. And they will become, afterwards, the soldiers. And they don't know what to do right now. And they're starting to speak about the miracles that happened to us during the days of exodus. So one of them points up to the mural on the wall and says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. When the Jewish people came out of Egypt-- this is on the right side-- God was with them. He helped them to get out of Egypt.

speaker: interviewee
text: But see what happened then. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. The [INAUDIBLE] and ran away. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Jordan turned backwards. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. The mountains were dancing like rams. And the end of these psalms, from the psalms, it is said in the Hallel during holidays, the end of it is [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. God who turns a rock into water, a rock into a well of water. So you see the illustration of this psalm. And the people are pointing to it. See, it happened. It's going to happen to us again. Some of them are not so sure. And there is one lady-- I believe that there are about 117 figures here. One lady, who cleaned up, took everything off the table. But what is left on the table is just a candle. And the candle is smoking, ah, telling us what is going to happen. And what really happened was that now the-- the students are coming to tell the rabbis that it's time-- it's morning time. But really, what they're saying is, it's very dangerous to stay now here anymore, because the Romans may find you. And if the Romans will find you, and they see that you are plotting against them, then you are going to be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rabbi Akiva was killed by the Romans later on. And 24,000 students of his died in a plague. That's the way it is described in the [INAUDIBLE]. But really, we think that they didn't die in a plague, between [INAUDIBLE] and-- and-- what is that? During the days of [INAUDIBLE], during the days when you count [INAUDIBLE]. But they were just killed by the Romans. The prophet Isaiah was the one who saw a different world at the end of days. As if the visions would be coming out of his brain, of his face, of the whole mentality of that prophet. What did he say? [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] They will take their swords and break them to plowshares, and they will go to the mountain of God. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. So here you have the vision. You have on the left side the fire, and the people are throwing their arm-- arm-- their arms into the fire. And here, all kinds of people from every nation are walking up to the mountain of God. Can you see on the right side, the one who is breaking the arrow that he has in his hand? And happy they are, because there is a world of peace that Otto wanted so much to see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rejoicing with the Torah. This is one of Otto Schneid's innovations. He made it-- he made a turnable picture. A picture that doesn't have up and down, sideways, whatever. You can hang this picture on the ceiling, you can put it on the floor, you can turn it. Either way. And it changes the feeling every time when you turn it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is here? A group of people. Men, women, children, flags. In the middle are-- as they turn on the side down below is the eternal light, two candles. And in the center is the eternal light. And the beauty of this picture is the movement. The people are dancing. This really should have been a picture that you hang, and you turn it slowly to see the figures, and to see how the figures change. Can we perhaps see it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSE FOR 6 SECONDS] And the joy, and the colors [PAUSE] of the dance with the Torah. [PAUSE FOR 6 SECONDS] Such a crowd. When you look at it from afar, you don't even realize how many figures are here. Every little light is a figure. [PAUSE FOR 13 SECONDS] Eternal light, and the candles [PAUSE] and the happiness.
